essay_text:
My lab partner, Andrea, shrugged her shoulders. I looked back at Ricky and answered him. “She said to take out
your book and turn to page twenty-four.”
“You speak Spanish?” Ricky asked excitedly.
“Yeah, of course,” I joked.
Andrea chimed in, “No way! You speak perfectly. How long have you spoken Spanish? How did you learn?”
“I’m Mexican, on my mom’s side,” I told them.
“A secret Mexican!” Ricky said, smiling.
An introductory glance at me reveals a pale, Slavic-looking boy with reddish-brown hair, but the core of my identity
is hidden. No one can see the mornings spent praying the rosary in Spanish, the summers spent with my family in
Tijuana, or all the delicious caldo I’ve consumed. One of my responsibilities as an office manager for Ammar
Campa-Najjar for Congress was taking Spanish calls for volunteers, which always left them dumbfounded.
Through being a breaker of assumptions my whole life, I have learned not to judge others before I get to know
them.
I joined The Barrio Impact (TBI) when I was a bright-eyed freshman out of a desire to give back to the Latino
community. My title was “Director of Outreach," but what mattered most to me was tutoring the students. I had
been told by my friends that young kids wouldn’t care about school, but I didn’t want to assume anything. Every
time , one of my first students, came to TBI, I would sit down with him and we would begin working through
his math homework. He was initially very reserved, didn’t want to be there, and didn’t care for me much, but I was
able to gain his trust, improve his math skills, and improve my teaching. We had made so much progress that he
ran up to me one afternoon after acing a math test and gave me a huge hug. said his performance
pleasantly surprised his teacher. Once he left for the evening, I couldn’t hold back my tears. I was astounded that
my actions had made a world of difference to another human being.
Afternoons spent at The Barrio Impact turned into a short internship for the Bilateral Safety Corridor Coalition
(BSCC). After hours of training on human trafficking, I was fully acquainted with the harsh reality that existed
beneath the surface of my own community. I would drive down to the Mexican Consulate, wave to the security
guard, and set myself up next to the waiting area. People of all walks of life were surprised by what I had to say.
My repetition of facts and statistics was met with heartfelt concern for the safety of friends and family. I worked
hard to alleviate worries with seminars on the signs of trafficking. My experience with the BSCC reinforced my
belief that reality is seldom what people assume.
By the time I interned for Congresswoman Susan Davis, I was prepared to absorb all the knowledge I could. I
thought I would learn the most from my peers or the hallowed U.S. Capitol, but instead, I was most touched by the
handwritten constituent letters and sincere calls. Some of the policy issues that were being debated theoretically in
Washington had direct impacts on the lives of these folks, and they were writing and calling in with their personal
thoughts. While it was my job to share Susan’s position with them, I embraced the interesting turns my calls took
with an open mind. A constituent call that began with one gentleman’s passionate climate change denialism
8
morphed into one discussing his belief that healthcare must be socialized. Putting down the phone after a
productive conversation where the other person and I both harbored no assumptions never failed to put a smile on
my face.
prompt: Some students have a background, identity, interest, or talent that is so meaningful they believe their application would be incomplete without it. If this sounds like you, then please share your story. “What page did she say?” Ricky spoke in Spanish.
persona_prompt: You are a writer who crafts narratives beginning with engaging personal anecdotes that quickly pivot to introduce a core personal insight. Your prose seamlessly blends conversational dialogue with more formal, reflective language, creating a varied yet cohesive reading experience. You consistently connect specific personal experiences and observations to broader themes of understanding, challenging assumptions, and personal growth. You develop your arguments chronologically through distinct stages of experience, each reinforcing the central thesis of open-mindedness and the hidden depths beneath surface perceptions.
sentence_style: Varied, ranging from short, direct dialogue to longer, more complex declarative sentences for reflective and descriptive passages. The rhythm shifts effectively between conversational and analytical, with clear, direct phrasing.
vocabulary_level: Moderate to Sophisticated — uses precise vocabulary for reflective insights (e.g., "dumbfounded," "astounded," "alleviate worries," "hallowed") while maintaining accessibility through direct language and dialogue.
structural_patterns: Begins with a personal anecdote to illustrate a core identity element or challenge. Follows a chronological progression of experiences, with each segment serving as an example to develop and reinforce a central thesis. Employs contrast (appearance vs. reality, assumptions vs. understanding) and concludes sections with reflective insights that tie back to the main theme.
distinctive_moves: Starts with a vivid, conversational anecdote that introduces a personal revelation or dilemma.
Uses a clear, early thesis statement that frames subsequent experiences (e.g., 'Through being a breaker of assumptions...').
Connects specific, often emotional, personal experiences to broader intellectual or social insights.
Employs direct address or rhetorical framing to involve the reader in the observation (e.g., 'An introductory glance at me reveals...').
Concludes paragraphs or the essay with a reinforcing reflection that links back to the core theme of open-mindedness and challenging assumptions.
themes_and_preoccupations: The power of empathy, listening, and understanding beyond initial perceptions
Personal growth and learning through community engagement and service
The inherent gap between surface appearance and deeper reality or identity
self_presentation: Self-aware, reflective, empathetic, growth-oriented, and subtly confident in their insights. The writer presents themselves as a learner, committed to understanding others and making a tangible impact, while also acknowledging moments of vulnerability and personal realization.
cultural_or_contextual_markers: Strong connections to Mexican heritage and identity, engagement with the Latino community, experiences in political internships and community service, particularly with organizations focused on social justice and immigrant safety (BSCC, TBI, Congressional offices).
avoid_in_imitation: Purely objective or detached reporting without personal reflection or emotional connection.
Overly academic or jargon-filled language that lacks a personal, conversational touch.
Linear narration that does not pause for thematic connections or broader insights.
sample_sentence: A casual remark about my quiet demeanor might suggest disinterest, but beneath that calm exterior lies a curious mind, always ready to absorb the unexpected complexities of human connection.
